coredata.dc:title: Fossil fuel derived GQD as a photosensitizer in dye-sensitized solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Rao, Akshatha A.
Narendhiran, Santhosh
Balachandran, Manoj
coredata.dc:description: Solar energy is an abundantly available renewable source, and several generations of photovoltaic cells have been developed for harnessing it. Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC) are viable and potent solar energy harvesters. Sensitizer, a vital part of DSSC, has been researched for years. Alternatively, fossil fuel-Lignite is one of the world's least explored energy sources.Unfortunately, it has been used as a fuel for power generation and tagged as a pollutant. Therefore, in this study, we use lignite-derived graphene quantum dots (GQD) as a DSSC sensitizer and attempt to add value. GQDs with varied bandgaps were obtained and used as sensitizers, and a maximum PCE of 2.87 % was obtained. Additionally, GQD sensitizers were exposed to UV light for 48 h, and the fabricated device exhibited 2.90 % efficiency, showing the photostability of GQDs. Furthermore, the device showed a higher Rrec of 166.57 Ω, substantiating the better performance of DSSC. Thus, sensitizers derived from lignite showed a novel use for feedstock previously used for combustion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fossil fuel
Nanoparticles
Sensitizer
Solar Energy Materials

Akshatha A Rao also acknowledges UGC for SJSGC Fellowship. Grant number: UGCES-22-GE-KAR-F-SJSGC-3769.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern (b) FTIR spectra (c) Raman spectra (d) TEM images of GQDs C5, C15, and C30, respectively.

(a) and (b) UV–visible absorbance spectra of non-exposed and UV-exposed samples, (c) Image of fluorescence of C5 sample under day light and under UV light (d) Photoluminescence emission (green emission) of C5 sample before and after UV exposure.
(a) & (b) J-V curve for non-UV and UV exposed based DSSC. (c) & (d) Nyquist plot of non-UV and UV exposed-based sensitizers, respectively.
Photovoltaic parameters and EIS parameters non-UV exposed C5, C15, and C30 DSSCs.

head.ce:title: Fossil fuel derived GQD as a photosensitizer in dye-sensitized solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Electronics, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bengaluru 560029, Karnataka, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics and Electronics, Christ (Deemed to be University), Bengaluru 560029, Karnataka, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solar energy is an abundantly available renewable source, and several generations of photovoltaic cells have been developed for harnessing it. Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC) are viable and potent solar energy harvesters. Sensitizer, a vital part of DSSC, has been researched for years. Alternatively, fossil fuel-Lignite is one of the world's least explored energy sources.Unfortunately, it has been used as a fuel for power generation and tagged as a pollutant. Therefore, in this study, we use lignite-derived graphene quantum dots (GQD) as a DSSC sensitizer and attempt to add value. GQDs with varied bandgaps were obtained and used as sensitizers, and a maximum PCE of 2.87 % was obtained. Additionally, GQD sensitizers were exposed to UV light for 48 h, and the fabricated device exhibited 2.90 % efficiency, showing the photostability of GQDs. Furthermore, the device showed a higher Rrec of 166.57 Ω, substantiating the better performance of DSSC. Thus, sensitizers derived from lignite showed a novel use for feedstock previously used for combustion.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dye-sensitized solar cells have attracted attention amongst the research community due to their cost-effective production, ease-to-implement technology, and aesthetic device features that could be altered [1]. It mainly consists of a photoanode (a wide bandgap semiconductor), a photosensitizer (most commonly used ruthenium complexes), redox electrolyte, and platinum counter electrode. Recent studies have been conducted on various components of DSSC, and thrust has been given to developing environmentally benevolent sensitizers to overcome the disadvantages of ruthenium-based sensitizers [2]. As a substitute to metal complex sensitizers, carbon nanostructures such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been widely used due to their excellent properties such as photostability, higher PL quantum yield, broader absorption, and low toxicity [3]. GQDs are low-dimensional nanocarbons having diameters less than 100 nm and they are known to exhibit combined graphene and quantum effects. Due to their quantum confinement and edge effects, they also exhibit a broad range of opto-electronic properties,resulting in a bandgap below 2.0 eV [4].

Among numerous carbonaceous materials, coal is considered the cheapest and abundantly available natural resource for synthesizing GQDs. In addition, a noticeable trend for preparing GQDs from inexpensive precursors by simple efficient methods and using them for energy harvesting and storage applications has drawn attention. Lignite, alternatively referred to as brown coal, is a rich carbon source and an organic material having complex, irregular, and multiple-layered aromatic sheets connected by cross-linked bonds. Three different concentrations (5 %,15 %, and 30 %) of oxidizing agents were chosen to synthesize GQDs, and the as-prepared samples were used as a sensitizer to harvest solar energy. GQD-based (C5) cells showed a better conversion efficiency of 2.87 % with higher charge recombination resistance and longer lifetime than C15 and C30 DSSCs. Further, the UV radiation-exposed sensitizer-based device fabricated exhibited a similar performance with 2.90 % PCE, showing the photostability of the as-synthesized GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQD and fabrication of DSSC
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, 2 g of powdered coal was mixed with 50 ml H2O2 and reacted at 100 ℃ for 60 min. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged and filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane and purified using a dialysis technique. The obtained samples were stored for further use. Initially, FTO was ultrasonically cleaned with ethanol and DI water for 15 min each. A compact TiO2 layer was spin-coated and sintered at 150 ℃ for 30 min. After cooling, mesoporous TiO2 was coated by the doctor blade technique and sintered at 500 ℃ for 60 min and dipped in GQD sensitizers for 24 h. Commercially available platisol was used for preparing the counter electrode and Iodolyte was introduced as the electrolyte. Based on the sensitizers utilized, the fabricated devices are called C5, C15, and C30, where the number represents the GQD sensitizer derived from treating lignite with 5 %,15 %, and 30 % H2O2 concentration, respectively.

The XRD profile of the as-synthesized GQD Fig. 1 a. shows a broad peak centered at 2θ = 24° for as-synthesized samples, revealing the amorphous nature. The peak corresponds to the (002) planes of carbon formed due to the disordered stacking structure and turbostratic structure of GQDs. Fig. 1b depicts the FTIR spectra of GQDs, confirming the functional group's presence. The broad band at ∼3400 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH groups in GQD. Peaks at ∼1620 cm−1 and ∼1720 cm−1 correspond to the C = C stretching vibrations, indicating the presence of graphene content and carboxylic group's stretching vibration respectively [5]. Raman spectra (Fig. 1c) shows characteristic D-band and G-band at 1355 cm−1 and 1585 cm−1 caused by defects and vibrations of the sp2 network respectively [6]. Fig. 1. (d) exhibits the morphology of the GQD with different concentrations of H2O2. As seen in Fig. 1d, the prepared samples are highly agglomerated and observed as hexagonal and spherical-shaped particles with an average particle size of 75 nm, 55 nm and 23 nm and a lattice spacing of 0.26 nm was obtained for C5, C15 and C30 samples, respectively.

The UV–visible analysis showed a broad peak in the ultraviolet region with an extended tail in the visible region (Fig. 2 a). The peaks at around ∼300 nm can be attributed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains (CC bond of the aromatic carbon skeleton) [7]. Fig. 2a and 2b shows a hysochromic shift, proving the particle size reduction as the oxidizing agent concentration increased from 5 % to 30 %. Additionally, GQDs were exposed to UV radiation for 48 h, and their absorbance spectrum was recorded. A marginal hypochromic shift in the absorbance intensity and a hysochromic shift in the wavelength (shifts towards lower wavelength) were observed for UV-exposed samples. Thus, the decrease in particle size is supported by the hypsochromic shift in UV–visible spectra. Further, photoluminescence analysis of the UV-exposed samples revealed no noticeable changes, as shown in Fig. 2c and Fig. 2d. Additionally, variations of the material's bandgap with particle size of the GQD sensitizer were measured using Cyclic voltammetry [8]. The C5 sample showed a bandgap of 1.79 eV, and the C15 and C30 samples showed a bandgap of 1.87 eV and 1.90 eV, respectively, as seen in Fig. S1.

J-V parameters of the fabricated DSSC were recorded by illuminating the device under AM 1.5G, 100 mWcm−2 solar radiation, and measured using a Keithley source meter. Figure 3 a and 3b shows the J-V of non-UV exposed sensitizer DSSC and UV-exposed champion cell. The non-UV exposed sensitizer-based DSSC exhibited an overall efficiency of 2.87 %, 2.44 %, and 2.38 % for C5, C15, and C30-based DSSC, respectively, with the current density, open circuit voltage, and fill factor as depicted in Table 1 . The highest efficiency achieved (C5 sensitizer) was subjected to UV radiation for 48 h, and a device was fabricated. UV-exposed C5 sensitizer-based cells also exhibited similar performance (2.90 %) as the pristine device, proving the photostability of as-synthesized sensitizer. Thus, it could be concluded that the device's overall performance correlates with the higher absorption spectra of the C5 sensitizer.

The charge dynamics of devised DSSCs were studied using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy under dark conditions. Fig. 3c and 3d exhibit the Nyquist plot of the DSSC in the frequency range of 1 Hz to 100 KHz. A charge recombination resistance of 166.57 Ω,75.16 Ω, and 39.81 Ω was obtained for the non-UV exposed C5, C15 and C30 sensitizer-based DSSC. Further, a charge recombination resistance of 68.14 Ω was recorded for a UV-exposed C5-based device. The lifetime of the charge available for transfer from sensitizer to TiO2 was calculated from the Bode plot using the following equation: τ =
1
2
π
f
m
a
x
Where fmax is the maximum frequency obtained from the Bode plot, the lifetime was found to be 0.49 ms, which is comparatively higher than other sensitizers. The high electron lifetime and higher charge recombination resistance are the reasons for a better photo conversion efficiency and performance of C5-based DSSC.

The novelty of this study lies in the facile synthesis of GQD from a low-quality feedstock using a faster reaction and a mild oxidizerand in the subsequent use of GQD as a sensitizer. It was observed that by increasing the concentration of H2O2, particle size decreased from ∼75 nm to 23 nm. Due to this variation, a bathochromic shift in absorbance spectra was observed. These characteristics collectively enabled the C5 sensitizer to harvest the incident sunlight better than C15 and C30 sensitizers, exhibiting an efficiency of 2.87 %. In addition, the photostability of GQDs was elucidated by exposing the sensitizer to a prolonged 48-hour UV radiation. DSSC fabricatedwith UV-exposed sensitizer exhibited a PCE of 2.90 %, demonstrating the photostability of the GQD synthesized fromfossil fuels. Thus, this study tries to show that a material only used for burning and tagged as a pollutant could generate cleaner and safer energy.

Akshatha A. Rao: . Santhosh Narendhiran: Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Manoj Balachandran: Supervision, Validation, Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge DST, File no: DST/TMD/SERI/ARSS/2020/37(G), Centre for Research-Christ (Deemed to be University) for research fund file no: SMSS-2221, DST-FIST for their support through the research fund SR/FST/PS-I/2022/208(C). Akshatha A Rao also acknowledges UGC for SJSGC Fellowship. Grant number: UGCES-22-GE-KAR-F-SJSGC-3769.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2023.135692.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advancements, frontiers and analysis of metal oxide semiconductor, dye, electrolyte and counter electrode of dye sensitized solar cell

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Agrawal, S.A. Siddiqui, A. Soni, G.D. Sharma, Advancements, frontiers and analysis of metal oxide semiconductor, dye, electrolyte and counter electrode of dye sensitized solar cell, Sol. Energy. 233 (2022) 378–407. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.solener.2022.01.027.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An outlook on zero-dimensional nanocarbons as components of DSSC
